title: Evas Gods
wikitext:
[]: Evas = Gods?             Quote Post by NemZ » Fri Jul 26, 2013 10:11 am Split from HERE . Bagheera wrote: This is actually something that irritates me. The Evas are not gods. They are humans shackled in armor of Lilin manufacture. That's it. They are powerful, yes, but they are not gods. Not even the Seeds of Life are gods. That said, they obviously aren't mecha, either. But since they're biological organisms with mechanical components grafted on they are cyborgs by definition. That's what the word means!

[]:             Quote Post by Stryker » Fri Jul 26, 2013 11:07 am God is an omnipotent, omniscient being if we take the religious jazz out of it.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Fri Jul 26, 2013 11:38 am NemZ wrote: That argument is pointless until you define 'gods' in a meaningful sense. Seele calls it a god, so clearly there's some room for discussion.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Fri Jul 26, 2013 11:45 am Bagheera wrote: Nah, they're rather specific about the fact they don't want to create a god.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Fri Jul 26, 2013 11:52 am NemZ wrote: Nevertheless, they do think an eva with both seeds would qualify.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Fri Jul 26, 2013 12:06 pm Actually the Titans didn't make themselves either. They were the children of the earth and the sky/space (hence the 'ancient aliens' deal) who were in turn children of higher orders of gods that quickly become more personified concepts than people, like Chaos and Nyx(night). And of course there's a huge difference between framing things as divine actions rather than the churning of a mechanistic universe, but for the purposes of the topic I'm not sure it's fair to require gods to be eternal beings (and depending on how you interpret 'quantum Rei', she might both have a beginning and exist at all times and places)

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Fri Jul 26, 2013 1:06 pm This is just a way of reframing the debate on whether Evangelion is a fantasy show about magic or science fiction show with a lot of mystical window dressing. This is, ultimately, a false question- it's a matter of semantics and the idea of magic in scientific terms is core to the show's internal mechanics and its themes. The Evas aren't gods, they're golems- complete with imagery of being shaped out of clay-like matter. They're part of the same storytelling tradition as Frankenstein's monster, and have commonalities with the alchemical homunculus. The Evas have a lot in common with Frankenstein, in that both capture the anxiety inherent in technological advancement- the fear that Man will use his technology to impinge on the province of God. It's an ancient story, going back to Genesis. The "fantasy or sci-fi" question inherent in this topic makes it impossible to answer. We moderns have imposed upon ourselves a real/unreal, natural/supernatural, mystical/mechanistic dichotomy that declares it impossible to reconcile magic with science. Eva rejects that, they're the same thing. The ascended Rei is a goddess, she is a being that is too large to fit into the four dimensional universe perceived by human beings- these are the same thing, put in different terms.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Fri Jul 26, 2013 1:55 pm NemZ wrote: Actually the Titans didn't make themselves either. They were the children of the earth and the sky/space (hence the 'ancient aliens' deal) who were in turn children of higher orders of gods that quickly become more personified concepts than people, like Chaos and Nyx(night).

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Fri Jul 26, 2013 2:15 pm Chuckman wrote: The "fantasy or sci-fi" question inherent in this topic makes it impossible to answer.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Fri Jul 26, 2013 2:20 pm Rei, if someone asks you if you're a god, you say yes .

[]:             Quote Post by CJD » Fri Jul 26, 2013 3:59 pm Chuckman wrote: Rei, if someone asks you if you're a god, you say yes .

[]:             Quote Post by Lavinius » Fri Jul 26, 2013 4:06 pm Like their parents the Seeds, they are near gods. However, evidently a being requires both Fruits to be officially a god, so only Yui-sama and the Adam/Lilith combination qualify. And Asuka, of course. She is definitely a goddess. This is, of course, using Seele's technical definition of a god. And no one has a clear definition of the word anyway. The Evas already far exceed many beings which have been called gods, but even if you want to have higher standards (that is, of cosmic significance rather than mere attributes) if you don't consider the Adam/Lilith to be a god then what the hell will you?

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Fri Jul 26, 2013 4:15 pm Chuckman wrote: Rei, if someone asks you if you're a god, you say yes .

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Fri Jul 26, 2013 5:19 pm I would say that Eva isn't a god until it undergoes 3I in EoE. Unless we're referring to it in that exact moment, it ain't no god.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Fri Jul 26, 2013 5:20 pm NemZ wrote: So what DOES count, and specifically why? What list of traits are you looking for in determining if some being can legitimately be described as a god?

[]:             Quote Post by Atropos » Fri Jul 26, 2013 5:44 pm ^ Under that definition, they're definitely gods. Gods of creation and destruction, I suppose, in the same way Shiva is referred to as both 'the Destroyer' and 'the Transformer.' hurr hurr he's a transformer

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Fri Jul 26, 2013 5:45 pm Atropos wrote: ^ Under that definition, they're definitely gods. Gods of creation and destruction, I suppose, in the same way Shiva is referred to as both 'the Destroyer' and 'the Transformer.' hurr hurr he's a transformer

[]:             Quote Post by Atropos » Fri Jul 26, 2013 5:48 pm ^ Eh? The Evas were intended (at least by Yui) to serve as controllers of Instrumentality; that makes their 'domain' destruction. And how does having a human soul not make them human?

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Fri Jul 26, 2013 5:56 pm Atropos wrote: ^ Eh? The Evas were intended (at least by Yui) to serve as controllers of Instrumentality; that makes their 'domain' destruction. And how does having a human soul not make them human?

[]:             Quote Post by Gob Hobblin » Thu Aug 01, 2013 12:41 pm Another interesting tangent to think on with this topic is what is meant as defined by a 'god' in this context. The anime itself was so filled with double-meaning and alternate definitions for terms, it would be interesting to ponder what an organization like Seele or a man like Gendo defined a 'god' as. It was clear that the people behind Instrumentality, if not especially religious, were certainly people of conviction. Scientific principal drove their actions to a point, but then rhetoric, ego, and ideology took over the rest of the way. With that in mind, what is their concept of 'god?' It's certainly a being of unimaginable power (possibly one that surpasses the Angels), but is that the only denominator? Perhaps there's also an element of free will that concerns them: as Bagheera just pointed out, the Evas were not intended to control instrumentality, merely facilitate it (allow others to control it). Perhaps they're idea of a god is simply a free-agent: a catalyst to Instrumentality that has it's own say in how it proceeds. That, of course, is probably wildly different from Yui's concept of what a god is, but still... Any thoughts?
